title: Two in the morning
summary: A locksmith arrives at two in the morning to help a young man who has lost his key. The young man asks why he still works nights.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: I came. Two in the morning. Show me the door.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: It's this one. I am sorry. I lost my key.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: Don't apologise. This is my job.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: Sixty euros. The price is the same at two in the morning as at two in the afternoon.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: Really. I do not like people who charge more at night. People are weaker at night. A weaker person should not pay a higher price.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: Hold this light, please. A little higher.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: Good. Now keep still. The lock is old. The pins are stiff.

body.speaker: Sam
body.text: Why do you still work at night?

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: I have a son. He is twenty-four. He lives in Madrid. He calls me at two in the morning sometimes. He needs nobody, mostly. But sometimes he needs someone. I like to know that if my phone rings at two, I am awake. Working at night keeps me awake at night.

body.speaker: Mr Devlin
body.text: There. The door is open. Sixty euros. And go to bed.

annotations.meaningVi: hầu hết thời gian / phần lớn là

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'should' and 'should not' for moral statements
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Should + động từ nguyên thể' đưa ra lời khuyên hoặc quan điểm về điều đúng. 'Should not' (ngắn: shouldn't) phủ định. Trong bài: 'A weaker person should not pay a higher price.' (quan điểm đạo đức). Lưu ý 'should' không thêm '-s' ở ngôi he/she/it: 'He should pay.' Mệnh lệnh thẳng dùng động từ nguyên thể, không có chủ ngữ: 'Don't apologise.' 'Go to bed.' Chúng mạnh hơn 'should not'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Should + base verb' gives advice or expresses an opinion about what is right. 'Should not' (shouldn't) is the negative. The passage: 'A weaker person should not pay a higher price.' (moral view). 'Should' never takes -s. Direct imperatives (no subject) like 'Don't apologise.' or 'Go to bed.' are stronger than 'should'.

translationVi:
Sam: A lô? Bác đến rồi.
Ông Devlin: Đến rồi. Hai giờ sáng. Cho tôi xem cánh cửa.
Sam: Cửa này ạ. Cháu xin lỗi. Cháu làm mất chìa.
Ông Devlin: Đừng xin lỗi. Đây là việc của tôi.
Sam: Có đắt không bác?
Ông Devlin: Sáu mươi euro. Giá hai giờ sáng bằng giá hai giờ chiều.
Sam: Thật ạ?
Ông Devlin: Thật. Tôi không thích những người tính giá cao hơn vào ban đêm. Ban đêm người ta yếu hơn. Người yếu hơn không nên trả giá cao hơn.
Sam: Cảm ơn bác.
Ông Devlin: Cầm cái đèn này, làm ơn. Đưa lên cao hơn một chút.
Sam: Vậy này ạ?
Ông Devlin: Tốt. Giờ giữ yên. Ổ khoá cũ. Chốt cứng.
Sam: Bao lâu vậy bác?
Ông Devlin: Bốn phút. Có khi năm.
Sam: Cháu hỏi một câu được không?
Ông Devlin: Một câu thôi nhé.
Sam: Sao bác vẫn làm đêm? Ý cháu là, ở tuổi bác.
Ông Devlin: Tôi có một đứa con trai. Hai mươi tư tuổi. Sống ở Madrid. Thỉnh thoảng nó gọi tôi lúc hai giờ sáng. Phần lớn thời gian nó không cần ai. Nhưng đôi khi nó cần một người. Tôi muốn biết rằng nếu điện thoại tôi reo lúc hai giờ, tôi đang thức. Làm đêm giữ tôi thức ban đêm.
Sam: Đó là lý do tốt.
Ông Devlin: Đây. Cửa mở rồi. Sáu mươi euro. Và đi ngủ đi.
Sam: Cảm ơn bác. Chúc bác ngủ ngon.
Ông Devlin: Chúc cháu ngủ ngon.

questions.prompt: What time is it in the story?
Why did Sam call Mr Devlin?
options: He wanted to buy a lock.
He could not find his phone.

questions.prompt: How much does Mr Devlin charge?
questions.hint: Mr Devlin gives the price directly.

questions.prompt: Why doesn't he charge more at night?
options: People are weaker at night; a weaker person should not pay a higher price.
He is in a good mood.
questions.hint: Mr Devlin gives a moral reason.

questions.prompt: What does he ask Sam to do during the work?
options: Hold the light and keep still
questions.explanation: 'Hold this light, please. A little higher.' / 'Now keep still.'

questions.prompt: How long will the work take?
Why does Mr Devlin still work at night?
options: His son in Madrid sometimes calls at two in the morning; he wants to be awake if the phone rings.
questions.explanation: Mr Devlin's long reply about his son.
questions.hint: Look at Mr Devlin's reply about his son.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Devlin tell Sam at the end?
What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: A working life can be quietly organised around the chance that someone you love might need you.
Locksmiths should always charge more at night.
Sons should not lose their keys.
questions.explanation: Mr Devlin shapes his work hours around being available for his son.
questions.hint: Think about why he keeps the schedule he does.

cloze.template:
Sam: Hello? You came.
Mr Devlin: I came. Two in the {{b1}}. Show me the door.
Sam: It's this one. I am sorry. I lost my {{b2}}.
Mr Devlin: Don't apologise. This is my job.
Sam: Will it be {{b3}}?
Mr Devlin: Sixty euros. The price is the same at two in the morning as at two in the afternoon.
Sam: Really?
Mr Devlin: Really. I do not like people who charge more at night. People are {{b4}} at night. A weaker person should not pay a higher price.
Sam: Thank you.
Mr Devlin: Hold this {{b5}}, please. A little higher.
Sam: Like this?
Mr Devlin: Good. Now keep still. The lock is old. The pins are {{b6}}.
Sam: How long will it take?
Mr Devlin: {{b7}} minutes. Maybe five.
Sam: Can I ask one thing?
Mr Devlin: One thing.
Sam: Why do you still work at night?
Mr Devlin: I have a son. He is twenty-four. He lives in {{b8}}. He calls me at two in the morning sometimes. He needs nobody, mostly. But sometimes he needs someone. I like to know that if my phone rings at two, I am awake. Working at night keeps me awake at night.
Sam: That is a good reason.
Mr Devlin: There. The door is {{b9}}. Sixty euros. And go to bed.
Sam: Thank you. Good night.
Mr Devlin: Good {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Devlin shapes his work hours around the possibility that his grown son might call at two in the morning. Is there a small choice in your own routine — when you sleep, when you keep your phone on, when you stay home — that you have quietly arranged around someone else's possible need?
